learning_objectives: Recognize a discrete-time signal as a sequence of numbers indexed by integers.
Distinguish continuous-time notation \(x(t)\) from discrete-time notation \(x[n]\).
Understand that a discrete-time system maps an input sequence \(x[n]\) to an output sequence \(y[n]\).
Connect uniform sampling of \(x(t)\) to the discrete-time identity \(x[n]=x(nT)\).
Apply the section's representative sampling example \(x(t)=e^{-t}\) with \(T=0.1\).

visual_plan.rationale: This section introduces notation and the visual idea of sampling. The exact definitions are best taught with LaTeX, while a standard stem-plot reference and a small interactive sampling demo make the difference between continuous-time curves and discrete-time sequences immediately visible. No textbook figure crop is available, so do not use a page screenshot.
visual_plan.cram: Use the stem plot and sampling demo to quickly identify whether a problem is asking about \(x(t)\), \(x[n]\), or \(x(nT)\).
visual_plan.standard: Use the visuals to connect the formula \(x[n]=x(nT)\) to one representative sampled exponential example.
visual_plan.top_score: Use the demo to notice that changing \(T\) changes which continuous-time points become the sequence, while the index \(n\) remains integer-valued.

blocks.instruction: Create the first lesson page as a compact outline only. Include exactly two short sections: 'Section Objective' and 'Concepts In This Section'. Under 'Section Objective', write one sentence: 'Understand how discrete-time signals are represented, processed by systems, and obtained by sampling continuous-time signals.' Under 'Concepts In This Section', list only these concept names as bullets: discrete-time signal, integer index \(n\), square-bracket notation \(x[n]\), discrete-time system, uniform sampling, sampling interval \(T\). Do not add examples, background paragraphs, exam tips, or expanded explanations on this page.

blocks.explanation_instruction: Start a new concept page with the heading '## 1. Discrete-time signals are sequences'. Explain in 90–130 words that a discrete-time signal is a sequence of numbers, not a smooth curve. State that \(n\) is an integer index such as \(...,-2,-1,0,1,2,...\), and \(x[n]\) means 'the value of sequence \(x\) at index \(n\)'. Include one minimal concrete example: if \(x[0]=3\), \(x[1]=5\), and \(x[2]=4\), then the sequence begins 3, 5, 4. Explain symbol meanings, when to use this notation, the exam trigger 'values listed at integer indices', and the common misuse: writing \(x(t)\) when the variable is an integer index. Avoid advanced signal-processing terminology.

blocks.search_query: Wikimedia Commons discrete-time signal stem plot sequence samples
blocks.purpose: Show a clean stem plot of a discrete-time signal so students see that values exist at separated integer indices rather than along a continuous curve.

mode_specific_visual_use.cram: Use the vertical stems as the fast recognition cue for a discrete-time sequence.
mode_specific_visual_use.standard: Use the figure to connect \(x[n]\) with indexed sample values.
mode_specific_visual_use.top_score: Use the separated stems to emphasize that the graph does not imply values between integer indices.

blocks.explanation_instruction: Start a new concept page with the heading '## 2. A discrete-time system processes a sequence'. Explain in 80–120 words that a discrete-time system takes an input sequence \(x[n]\) and produces an output sequence \(y[n]\). Use a digital computer as the familiar example, matching the textbook. Clarify that the system acts on numbers indexed by integers, not on a continuously varying signal. Include one representative example without computation: a system could take daily temperatures \(x[n]\) and output a smoothed daily temperature sequence \(y[n]\). Explain when to use this input-output picture, the exam trigger 'input sequence produces output sequence', and the common misuse: thinking \(y[n]\) is one number instead of an entire output sequence.

Start a new concept page with the heading '## 3. Sampling turns \(x(t)\) into \(x[n]\)'. Explain in 100–150 words that if a continuous-time signal \(x(t)\) is sampled every \(T\) seconds, the discrete-time value at index \(n\) is the value of the original signal at time \(t=nT\). Define every symbol: \(x(t)\) is the continuous-time signal, \(T\) is the sampling interval, \(n\) is an integer, \(x(nT)\) is the sampled value, and \(x[n]\) is the discrete-time notation for that value. Include the exam trigger 'sampled every \(T\) seconds' and the common misuse: treating \(n\) as seconds instead of an index. Keep the tone foundational.

blocks.title: Sampling a continuous-time curve
blocks.purpose: Let students see how a smooth curve becomes a discrete-time sequence when values are taken only at \(t=nT\).

demo_spec.layout: Show one coordinate panel. Draw a smooth continuous curve \(x(t)=e^{-t}\) in light gray. Overlay sampled points and vertical stems at \(t=nT\) in navy. Label the horizontal axis \(t\) and show small integer labels \(n=0,1,2,...\) near the sample points.
demo_spec.student_task: Ask the student to move \(T\) and notice that larger \(T\) gives fewer sampled points over the same time window, while the index \(n\) still counts samples as integers.
what_to_notice: The gray curve is \(x(t)\), which exists for continuous time.
The navy stems are the discrete-time sequence values.
\(x[n]\) is shorthand for the sample taken at \(t=nT\).

mode_specific_visual_use.cram: Use the slider to memorize the exam trigger: sampling interval \(T\) means evaluate at \(t=nT\).
mode_specific_visual_use.standard: Use the demo to understand why \(x[n]=x(nT)\) is a notation bridge, not a new signal formula from nowhere.
mode_specific_visual_use.top_score: Use the demo to separate the index \(n\), sample time \(nT\), and sample value \(x(nT)\).

blocks.explanation_instruction: Continue the sampling page with the textbook's representative example. Explain in 70–100 words that the continuous-time signal is \(x(t)=e^{-t}\). Sampling means substitute \(t=nT\), so \(e^{-t}\) becomes \(e^{-nT}\). State that this is not a new exponential law; it is direct substitution of the sample time. Include the symbol meanings, when to use this formula, and the common trap: substituting \(n\) for \(t\) and forgetting the factor \(T\).

Finish the example by setting \(T=0.1\) seconds. Explain in 60–90 words that because the sampling interval is \(0.1\), the sample time is \(t=0.1n\), so \(x(nT)=e^{-0.1n}\). Include one quick check: at \(n=0\), the value is \(e^0=1\); at \(n=1\), the value is \(e^{-0.1}\). State the exam note: always substitute the given \(T\) before simplifying the discrete-time expression.

blocks.instruction: Create a recap page titled '📌 Key Takeaways'. Include exactly 4 bullets, each ≤25 words, and include the core formulas explicitly. Required formulas to include: \(x[n],\; n\in\mathbb{Z}\); \(x[n]=x(nT)\); \(x(nT)=e^{-nT}\); \(x(nT)=e^{-0.1n}\) for \(T=0.1\). Also include the input-output idea \(x[n]\to\text{system}\to y[n]\). End with one bridge sentence: 'Next, we will use these basic discrete-time ideas to analyze how systems respond to input sequences.'

knowledge_points.label: Discrete-time signal as an indexed sequence

questions.stem: Which description best matches a discrete-time signal?
options: A. A smooth curve defined for every real value of time
B. A sequence of numbers indexed by integer values
C. A single number produced by a digital computer
D. A continuous signal that cannot be sampled
questions.explanation: A discrete-time signal is a sequence of values indexed by integers such as \(n=0,1,2,...\).

wrong_option_explanations.A: That describes a continuous-time signal, usually written with parentheses such as \(x(t)\).
wrong_option_explanations.C: A sequence contains many indexed values, not just one number.
wrong_option_explanations.D: Sampling is one common way to create a discrete-time signal.

questions.stem: In the notation \(x[n]\), what does \(n\) represent?
options: A. Any real-valued time variable
B. The sampling interval
C. An integer index into the sequence
D. The output of the system
questions.explanation: \(n\) is an integer index. It tells which entry of the sequence is being referenced.

wrong_option_explanations.A: A real-valued time variable is usually written as \(t\), not \(n\).
wrong_option_explanations.D: The output sequence is usually written \(y[n]\).

questions.hint: The square brackets are the clue.

questions.stem: Which notation is the best match for a continuous-time signal?
questions.explanation: The textbook reserves parentheses for continuous-time variables such as \(t\), so \(x(t)\) is continuous-time notation.

wrong_option_explanations.C: \(y[n]\) is also discrete-time notation, usually for an output sequence.
wrong_option_explanations.D: \(nT\) is a sample time, not the signal notation itself.

questions.stem: A discrete-time system takes \(x[n]\) and produces \(y[n]\). What are \(x[n]\) and \(y[n]\)?
options: A. One input number and one output number
B. Two continuous-time curves
C. An input sequence and an output sequence
D. The sampling interval and sample time
questions.explanation: A discrete-time system processes a whole input sequence \(x[n]\) to produce a whole output sequence \(y[n]\).

wrong_option_explanations.A: Each notation refers to a sequence, not just one isolated number.
wrong_option_explanations.B: The square brackets indicate discrete-time signals, not continuous-time curves.
wrong_option_explanations.D: The sampling interval is \(T\), and the sample time is \(nT\).

questions.hint: Think input sequence \(\to\) system \(\to\) output sequence.

questions.stem: A continuous-time signal \(x(t)\) is sampled every \(T\) seconds. Which formula correctly connects the sampled sequence to the original signal?
questions.explanation: Uniform sampling evaluates the continuous-time signal at sample times \(t=nT\), so \(x[n]=x(nT)\).

wrong_option_explanations.C: This mixes continuous-time and discrete-time notation incorrectly.
wrong_option_explanations.D: Sampling does not mean multiplying the signal value by \(T\).

questions.hint: The sample time is index times interval.

questions.stem: Observe a sampling demo: when the sampling interval \(T\) is increased, what happens over the same time window?
options: A. More sample points appear because the index \(n\) increases faster
B. Fewer sample points appear because the samples are farther apart in time
C. The continuous-time curve disappears
D. The values stop being a sequence
questions.explanation: Larger \(T\) means sample times \(t=nT\) are spaced farther apart, so fewer samples fit in the same time window.

wrong_option_explanations.A: Increasing \(T\) spaces samples farther apart, not closer together.
wrong_option_explanations.C: The original continuous-time curve still exists; sampling only selects points from it.
wrong_option_explanations.D: The sampled values are still indexed by integers, so they still form a sequence.

questions.hint: Watch the horizontal spacing between stems.

questions.stem: Given \(x(t)=e^{-t}\) and sampling interval \(T=0.1\), write the sampled signal \(x(nT)\). Show the substitution step.
questions.explanation: This checks whether the student understands sampling as substitution into the continuous-time expression.
questions.hint: Replace every \(t\) with \(nT\), then plug in \(T=0.1\).

section_specific_rules:
section_specific_rules:
- This section is a conceptual introduction, not a full computational drill.
- Use exam-priority guidance only as a light weighting signal.
- Keep the core identity of the section intact; do not let worked examples or trap warnings dominate the lesson.
- Include at most one representative worked example unless the OCR strongly suggests more.
